results.title: The Human Reach is rated "Excellent" with 4.6 / 5 on Trustpilot
results.content:
to an EVP. I'm sure the process would have worked even better and faster had I fully engaged in the "side door" method, but the point is: Career Amp works for you and not your prior employer. So, they help you figure out who you really are and prepare you to confidently reach for something better. [...] On the Sidedoor Method, you said it’s “cool” but questioned the value. Fair question. Here’s the harder one. Most job seekers never go outbound. They send resumes into the void for months. The ones who reach out directly hire faster. You went outbound. You hired faster. So the question isn’t whether the method is worth $7K. It’s whether landing the role faster is worth it. That’s the call every client makes before they sign.

On AI, we never sold our outreach strategy as an “AI mastermind.” Our tools automate LinkedIn connection requests inside the Sidedoor strategy. That’s all it’s ever been. The AI work lives in asset writing, message scripting, and interview prep. Those weren’t hidden. They were waiting. [...] Worse 7 thousand dollars I’ve ever spent. This company offered meagre guidance and support for re-routing my career or providing any tangible options. It’s essentially a resume and cover letter re-writing service dressed up as a business that will work with you until you land a job. I did land a job, but it was thanks to my diligence of continuing to send out messages and write my own job apps. Human reach just considered themselves a glorified partner in the act. What took place was frankly sinister. They prey on people who had successful careers and have capital to spend in landing their perfect role. Ya I guess the side door method (messaging people on LinkedIn) is cool, but is sharing that as what you should do worth 7k really? Also they sold me on the help of AI. Then I join and all

results.title: 9 Mistakes To Avoid In Your Physician Referral Network
results.content:
Malpractice Insurance
Malpractice Insurance

Take the case of The California Medical Association. They’re the largest physician group in the state and back in 2012, along with 60 individual doctors, were involved in a lawsuit claiming that Aetna had illegally threatened patients and doctors who wanted to use out-of-network medical providers.

These threats left patients facing high out-of-pocket costs for going out of network, and undoubtedly frustrated and dissatisfied. While this example included doctors, there are many elements of patient-insurance interaction that you will not be privy to, and that can taint your patients’ care experience and consequently, their opinion of you as a provider. [...] Your network impacts elements of your work including income stability, the satisfaction if your patients, even information security. That’s why it’s crucial to build a strong referral network in strategic ways from the very beginning (we’ve put together some tips on that here.)

That’s not the end though. Once your network is established, it requires maintenance, and there are a few, key mistakes that can break the quality of your referral network and the well-being of your practice overall.

healthcare referrals

healthcare referrals

## 1. Failing To Monitor Patient Satisfaction

While there are multiple ways to evaluate the health of your network (we’ll discuss some in a few), patient satisfaction is arguably the most important to keep an eye on. [...] To address this, keep up with your overall patient experience. Conduct surveys and if any warnings pop up (such as a patient stuck in a particularly difficult situation with an insurer), investigate what’s actually going on. Remember, not every patient that’s having an issue will speak up and getting a feel for your patient group experience may take some proactive work on your part. Additionally, make sure that your staff is trained to flag issues with payors and other providers if they are brought up, even in casual situations by your patients.

## 2. Ignoring Audit Trails

If you do end up in a malpractice situation or even a simple dispute over patient experiences, a concise and informative audit trail will be essential to things working out in your favor.

results.title: Physician Network Referral Management | HSG Advisors
results.content: 1) Providers Directly-Referring Out. This most frequently occurs when providers deliberately refer out due to previously established relationships, access issues, or quality concerns with in-network providers and/or resources. This can also occur accidentally when employed referring providers are confused by who is part of their network, and who is not. Front office staff also contribute to the leakage; when tasked with making a referral, they take the path of least resistance to securing a referral appointment. [...] 2) Developing a Prioritized Plan. With the right data in place, a comprehensive strategy will focus on baseline improvement. Strategy should prioritize improvement opportunities based on strategic and financial importance. Building an action plan to address “right-to-win” issues is important, as well, as no amount of provider engagement will influence providers to refer to poor quality services and those difficult to access. [...] 2) Patients Self-Referring Out. This often occurs when a competitor has built a strong brand or market presence and is able to influence patient perception. Recommendations of friends and family, including those solicited via social media, also play a role. This can show up as the patient asking for a non-employed provider or ignoring the provider’s referral and making their own choice. And, more and more, payers are getting involved in the decision-making process, influencing where patients receive care.
